message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The basic reproductive number (R
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  ) is a function of contact rates among individuals, transmission probability, and duration of infectiousness. We sought to determine the association between population density and R
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  of SARS-CoV-2 across U.S. counties, and whether population density could be used as a proxy for contact rates. We conducted a cross-sectional analysis using linear mixed models with random intercept and fixed slopes to assess the association of population density and R
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  . We also assessed whether this association was differential across county-level main mode of transportation-to-work percentage. Counties with greater population density have greater rates of transmission of SARS-CoV-2, likely due to increased contact rates in areas with greater density. The effect of population density and R
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  was not modified by private transportation use. Differential R
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  by population density can assist in more accurate predictions of the rate of spread of SARS-CoV-2 in areas that do not yet have active cases.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Article Summary Line</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>U.S. counties with greater population density have greater rates of transmission of SARS-CoV-2, likely due to increased contact rates in areas with greater density.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Population density and basic reproductive number of COVID-19 across United States counties
